title: Tech Dining Demand Forecasting and Optimisation
summary: Developed demand models and simulation logic for West Village Hall dining operations, linking menu-level choice behavior to planning decisions, waste reduction, and service-station demand sensing.

course.name: Production planning for West Village Hall dining operations

highlights: Engineered discrete-choice demand models for 1,342 menu items using multinomial logit scoring.
Reduced hall waste from 44 percent to 13 percent through better planning logic.
Created a SimPy-based demand-sensing engine for 1,500-plus peak daily footfall across five stations.

sections.heading: Operational question
body: The project focused on dining-hall production planning, where waste and service performance depend heavily on demand uncertainty. The key problem was to move from reactive food preparation toward a better demand-aware planning model.
That required modeling choice behavior at a granular level rather than forecasting total footfall alone.

sections.heading: Choice modeling
body: A multinomial-logit-style scoring approach was used across 1,342 menu items to capture how menu options compete for demand. This shifted the problem from broad aggregate estimation to item-level demand structure.
The point of that detail was to support production decisions closer to actual menu behavior.

sections.heading: Demand sensing and station flow
body: A SimPy-based demand-sensing engine was then used to examine peak daily footfall across five dining stations. That exposed a 109 percent higher protein-demand pattern than initial assumptions suggested, which is exactly the kind of operational signal that simple averages often miss.
The simulation layer therefore complemented the choice model by showing how station-level activity behaves in time.

sections.heading: Outcome
body: The work reported a reduction in hall waste from 44 percent to 13 percent, which made the project immediately legible as an operations-improvement case study. It also demonstrates the Georgia Tech theme of turning analytical models into decisions with real planning consequences.
As the folder grows, this page can hold the actual model assumptions, datasets, and planning outputs without any routing changes.
